last_updated: 2026-04-20 (+class_color per mineral, 12-hue color wheel for topo map)

_schema.max_nucleation_count: Maximum number of crystals of this species that can ever nucleate in a single run. Counted across the whole simulation (active + dissolved). Keeps common minerals like wulfenite from producing microcrystalline swarms that get immediately swallowed by other phases.
_schema.max_size_cm: Hard cap on crystal size — 2x world record per research/mineral-accuracy-audit.md. Growth halts when reached; this is the fix for the 321248% bug.
_schema.growth_rate_mult: Relative growth rate, calcite=1.0 baseline. Multiply the runtime base rate.
_schema.T_range_C: [min, max] temperature window for growth (C). null,null = unbounded.
_schema.pH_dissolution_below: pH below which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.pH_dissolution_above: pH above which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.required_ingredients: {species: min_ppm}. All must be present for growth.
_schema.thermal_decomp_C: Temperature above which mineral breaks down (C).
_schema.fluorescence: {activator, threshold_ppm, color, quencher} or null if non-fluorescent.
_schema.twin_laws: [{name, miller_indices, trigger, probability}] or [] if none known.
_schema.acid_dissolution: {pH_threshold, reaction, products} or null if acid-stable.
_schema.habit_variants: Array of habit objects. Each has: name, wall_spread (0-1, lateral spread on wall), void_reach (0-1, projection into vug), vector (projecting|coating|tabular|equant|dendritic), trigger (conditions that favor this habit). Growth engine selects habit based on σ, T, and space.
_schema.class: Mineralogical class for filtering/library UI: sulfide | carbonate | oxide | silicate | sulfate | phosphate | hydroxide | halide | arsenate | molybdate | tungstate | native.
_schema.description: 2-3 sentence geological description for library mode. Learning-audience voice; not flavor text.
_schema.scenarios: Array of scenario IDs (from SCENARIOS registry) in which this mineral can nucleate. Empirically verified with seed 42.
_schema.narrate_function: Name of dedicated _narrate_* function. null = inline flavor, needs refactor.
_schema.runtimes_present: Files where growth engine currently exists.
_schema.class_color: Hex color for topographic map visualization. Derived from mineral class — all classes of the same type share one hue on a 12-color wheel (30° spacing). Wall/matrix = #D2691E (amber).

_audit_summary._note: Post-phase-2 state. The _initial_audit block below is frozen at phase-1 start for historical reference — do not edit. +topaz (MG-B), +tourmaline/beryl/spodumene (MG-A), +anglesite/cerussite/pyromorphite/vanadinite (Pb oxidation), +bornite/chalcocite/covellite (Cu sulfide enrichment), +cuprite/azurite/native_copper (Cu oxide-native), +magnetite/lepidocrocite/stibnite/bismuthinite/native_bismuth/clinobisvanite (Fe/Sb/Bi suite), +chrysocolla (Bisbee), +marcasite (round-2: pH<5 pyrite dimorph), +wurtzite (round-2: T>95 sphalerite dimorph), +erythrite/annabergite (round-2: paired vivianite-group arsenates), +tetrahedrite/tennantite (round-2: paired fahlore Sb/As sulfosalts), +apophyllite (round-2: zeolite-facies silicate, hematite-phantom 'bloody apophyllite' habit, deccan_zeolite scenario added).

_initial_audit._note: Frozen snapshot of the pre-phase-1 audit. For historical reference only.

habit_variants.name: scepter_overgrowth
habit_variants.trigger: σ pulse after initial prism growth

quartz.description: The most abundant vug-lining mineral on Earth. Silica dissolved in hot fluid crystallizes on cooling — clear, smoky when irradiated, amethyst when Fe-bearing and irradiated together. The canvas on which most other vug minerals grow.
quartz.thermal_decomp_reaction: SiO2 melting point — takes hell itself

habit_variants.name: flos-ferri_acicular
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ, thin needle growth

calcite.description: Retrograde solubility makes calcite the giant-maker of vugs: hotter fluid holds less Ca+CO₃, so heating precipitates calcite. Fizzes in acid. Mn²⁺ activates orange fluorescence; Fe²⁺ quenches it — the boundary between glowing and dark zones records a fluid-chemistry shift.
calcite.audit_status: ok — most complete implementation, use as template

sphalerite.description: ZnS, the dominant zinc ore. Classic MVT mineral — forms at 60-200°C where metal brines meet H₂S-bearing groundwater. Iron substitution darkens the color from pale yellow (low-Fe cleiophane) through honey-brown to nearly black marmatite.

acid_dissolution.reaction: ZnS + 2H+ -> Zn2+ + H2S (slow, only in strong acid)

habit_variants.name: hemimorphic_crystal
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — hexagonal pyramid with polar c-axis, one end pointed one end flat

habit_variants.name: radiating_columnar
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — radiating hexagonal columns in stellate clusters

habit_variants.name: fibrous_coating
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ, fast growth — fibrous crust on fracture walls

habit_variants.name: platy_massive
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — tabular {0001} plates, micaceous aggregates

wurtzite.description: (Zn,Fe)S, the hexagonal high-temperature dimorph of sphalerite — same composition, different crystal structure. Above 95°C, cubic sphalerite gives way to hexagonal wurtzite with the distinctive hemimorphic habit (one end pointed, one end flat, due to the non-centrosymmetric P6₃mc space group). On cooling below 95°C, wurtzite converts to sphalerite — but sphalerite rarely goes back. The asymmetric dimorphism means finding wurtzite is diagnostic of rapid cooling from the hexagonal field.

trace_ingredients.Fe: substitutes for Zn, darkens color (same as sphalerite, but hotter so more Fe uptake)

acid_dissolution.reaction: (Zn,Fe)S + 2H+ → Zn2+ + H2S (slow, only in strong acid — same as sphalerite)

wurtzite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: T>95 sphalerite dimorph, hexagonal P6₃mc, hemimorphic habit, polymorphic inversion handled).

fluorite.description: CaF₂, a cubic halide with retrograde solubility. Classic MVT companion to sphalerite and galena. The word 'fluorescent' derives from fluorite — blue-violet under both LW and SW UV, driven by REE substitution and radiation-damage color centers.

pyrite.description: FeS₂, fool's gold. Enormous T range (25-700°C), always reducing. Oxidation produces goethite/limonite plus sulfuric acid — the acid-mine-drainage reaction. Three common habits: cubic with striated faces, 12-face pyritohedral, and raspberry-shaped framboidal aggregates from sedimentary microbial sulfate reduction.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — pyrite + O2 + H2O -> Fe2+ + SO4 + H+ (acid mine drainage)

habit_variants.name: cockscomb
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, acid fluid — tabular plates crested like a rooster's comb

habit_variants.name: spearhead
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — swallowtail {101} twins with pyramidal terminations

habit_variants.name: radiating_blade
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T (<100°C) — fibrous stellate sprays from a common center

habit_variants.name: tabular_plate
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, slow growth — flat {010} plates

marcasite.description: FeS₂, the orthorhombic dimorph of pyrite — same formula, different crystal structure. Acidic (pH<5), low-T (<240°C) fluids switch FeS₂ from cubic pyrite to orthorhombic marcasite. Diagnostic cockscomb and spearhead habits, paler brass than pyrite with iridescent tarnish. Metastable: converts to pyrite over geologic time or on heating, and rots in humid air to sulfuric acid and iron sulfate (the classic museum-specimen problem).
marcasite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic FeS2 → cubic FeS2 (pyrite) — metastable inversion, not true decomposition

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative breakdown in humid air — FeS2 + O2 + H2O → FeSO4 + H2SO4 (museum rot); extreme acid dissolves directly

nucleation_preferences.note: Acid-loving FeS2 dimorph. In MVT deposits marcasite often coats earlier sphalerite/galena. Subsurface goethite/limonite pseudomorphs after marcasite are the classic Egyptian Prophecy Stones.

marcasite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: pH<5 & T<240 dimorph of pyrite, full habit set, metastable inversion handled).

chalcopyrite.description: CuFeS₂, the primary copper ore. Forms in porphyry systems at 200-600°C, peaking 300-500°C during K-silicate alteration. Its oxidation feeds malachite, azurite, and the whole secondary copper-carbonate paragenesis. Tarnished surfaces show iridescent 'peacock ore' colors.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative — CuFeS2 + O2 -> Cu2+ + Fe3+ + SO4 (leads to malachite pathway)

hematite.description: Fe₂O₃, the ferric iron oxide. Habit changes with T: specular-platy above 300°C, botryoidal kidney-ore below 150°C, earthy red-ochre at surface. The rosette iron-rose is a metamorphic-hydrothermal classic from the Swiss Alps.

habit_variants.name: botryoidal
habit_variants.trigger: default — Cu²⁺ + CO₃²⁻, nearly always coating

malachite.description: Cu₂(CO₃)(OH)₂, the green copper carbonate. Low-T supergene — forms where chalcopyrite oxidizes in CO₂-rich groundwater. Banded botryoidal masses have been prized for decoration since the Bronze Age, from Egyptian amulets to Russian palace columns.
malachite.audit_status: max_size is for single-crystal; botryoidal aggregates can exceed this

uraninite.description: UO₂, the primary uranium mineral. Pitch-black, extremely dense, radioactive. Forms under strongly reducing conditions — any oxygen converts it to yellow secondary uranium minerals. Each decay turns a U atom into Pb; old uraninite accumulates radiogenic lead that eventually precipitates as nearby galena.

fluorescence.status: newly_added — uraninite itself weakly fluoresces, daughter minerals strongly

uraninite.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes). Emits radiation each step.

galena.description: PbS, the densest common sulfide (SG 7.6). Perfect cubic cleavage — tap it and it shatters into tiny cubes. Argentiferous galena was the historic source of most of the world's silver: Potosí, Leadville, Broken Hill.
galena.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes). T_optimum 100-400°C per mineral-accuracy-audit.md.

smithsonite.description: ZnCO₃, zinc's carbonate heir: forms when sphalerite oxidizes in CO₂-bearing groundwater. Named for James Smithson, founder of the Smithsonian. Botryoidal habit is typical; trace Cu gives prized turquoise-blue, Co gives pink, Cd gives yellow.
smithsonite.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes).

habit_variants.name: thin_plate_honey
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, rapid growth, very thin

wulfenite.description: PbMoO₄, the sunset caught in stone. Thin tabular plates in honey-yellow to blood-orange. Requires BOTH galena AND molybdenite to have oxidized in the same oxidation zone (Seo et al. 2012) — a narrow paragenetic window that makes specimens collector-prized.
wulfenite.audit_status: ok (phase-2: wired in vugg.py, narrators in all 3 runtimes). Requires both galena AND molybdenite to have oxidized first (Seo et al. 2012).

molybdenite.description: MoS₂, the primary molybdenum ore. Soft hexagonal platy crystals (Mohs 1-1.5), bluish-gray, greasy — looks like graphite but with a greenish streak. In porphyry systems Mo arrives in a SEPARATE pulse from Cu (Bingham Canyon, Seo et al. 2012), a late magmatic fluid delivering molybdenum on its own timeline.

adamite.description: Zn₂(AsO₄)(OH), a supergene arsenate from oxidizing Zn-As sulfide ores. Classic Ojuela Mine (Mexico) habit: yellow-green prismatic sprays. Cu-substituted zones (cuproadamite) fluoresce brilliant apple-green under SW UV — but heavy Cu quenches the glow, creating striking fluorescent/non-fluorescent crystal pairs.

mimetite.description: Pb₅(AsO₄)₃Cl — the 'mimic,' so named because it's isostructural with pyromorphite and distinguishable only by chemistry. Hexagonal prisms and the famous campylite barrels (Cumbria). Supergene product of galena oxidation in As-bearing groundwater.

habit_variants.name: cobalt_bloom
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — earthy crimson-pink crust, the field bloom

habit_variants.name: bladed_crystal
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — rare striated {010} blades, transparent crimson-pink

habit_variants.name: radiating_fibrous
habit_variants.trigger: grown on primary Co-arsenide substrate — stellate fibrous sprays

erythrite.description: Co₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O — the crimson-pink 'cobalt bloom' of medieval miners. A low-T supergene arsenate formed by oxidation of primary cobalt arsenides (cobaltite, skutterudite). Shares the vivianite-group structure with annabergite (Ni), and forms a continuous solid solution: Ni substitution for Co pales the color from crimson-pink through purplish-pink toward apple-green. Dehydrates above 200°C.
erythrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Co3(AsO4)2·8H2O → Co3(AsO4)2 (loses 8 lattice waters; lattice collapses)

acid_dissolution.reaction: erythrite + 6H+ → 3 Co²⁺ + 2 H3AsO4 + 8 H2O (dissolves in moderately acidic fluids)

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with annabergite (Ni equivalent). The vivianite-group sibling — same structure, different metal. Classic Schneeberg and Bou Azzer association directly on weathering Co-arsenides.

erythrite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with annabergite, low-T oxidizing arsenate, solid-solution color modeling).

habit_variants.name: nickel_bloom
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — apple-green earthy crust, the field bloom

habit_variants.name: capillary_crystal
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ — rare hair-like capillary fibers

habit_variants.name: cabrerite
habit_variants.trigger: Mg-substituted — pale green to white (Sierra Cabrera variety)

habit_variants.name: co_bearing
habit_variants.trigger: Co present — pinkish-green intermediate along erythrite-annabergite solid solution

annabergite.description: Ni₃(AsO₄)₂·8H₂O — 'nickel bloom,' the apple-green Ni equivalent of erythrite. Same vivianite-group structure, same supergene origin; nickel substitutes for cobalt and the color shifts from crimson to apple-green. Mg substitution produces the pale cabrerite variety; Co substitution pinks it toward erythrite. Dehydrates above 200°C.

trace_ingredients.Co: solid-solution toward erythrite (color shift to pinkish-green)
trace_ingredients.Mg: Mg-bearing cabrerite variety (pale green to white)

annabergite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Ni3(AsO4)2·8H2O → Ni3(AsO4)2 (loses 8 lattice waters; lattice collapses)

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with erythrite (Co equivalent). Apple-green crust on weathering Ni-arsenides. The solid-solution with erythrite means field-identification by color is diagnostic of Ni/Co ratio.

annabergite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with erythrite, same vivianite-group structure, low-T oxidizing arsenate).

habit_variants.name: crustiform
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, on fracture walls — banded crust

habit_variants.name: druzy_coating
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ — fine-grained drusy microcrystals

tetrahedrite.description: Cu₁₂Sb₄S₁₃ — the Sb-endmember 'fahlore,' German miner's term for the steel-gray copper sulfosalts that paid in silver as much as in copper. Hydrothermal 100-400°C, optimum 200-300°C. Cubic I4̄3m space group produces diagnostic {111} tetrahedra. Paired with tennantite (As endmember) in a continuous solid solution: the Sb:As ratio of an ore fluid is recorded directly in the composition. Ag substitutes for Cu to produce the silver ore variety 'freibergite.'

trace_ingredients.Ag: freibergite variety — silver ore (up to 18% Ag)

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — Cu²⁺ + Sb³⁺ released (→ secondary Sb oxides, malachite/azurite downstream)

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with tennantite (As endmember). Classic porphyry-to-epithermal transition mineral — grows on earlier chalcopyrite as the fluid cools and Sb activity climbs.

tetrahedrite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with tennantite, same cubic structure, Sb endmember fahlore).

habit_variants.name: tetrahedral
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — gray-black tetrahedra (indistinguishable from tetrahedrite except by the cherry-red thin-edge transmission test)

habit_variants.name: crustiform
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, fracture-wall — banded gray-black crust

habit_variants.name: druzy_coating
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ — fine-grained drusy microcrystals

tennantite.description: Cu₁₂As₄S₁₃ — the As-endmember fahlore, paired with tetrahedrite. Same cubic I4̄3m structure; arsenic replaces antimony. Visually indistinguishable from tetrahedrite except by the cherry-red thin-fragment transmission test (tennantite transmits; tetrahedrite stays opaque). On oxidation releases Cu + AsO₄³⁻, feeding the secondary arsenate paragenesis: adamite, erythrite, annabergite, mimetite. Closes the loop between primary copper sulfosalts and downstream supergene arsenates.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — Cu²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ released (→ adamite, erythrite, annabergite, mimetite downstream)

cherry_red_transmission.trigger: thin edge under strong backlight — diagnostic for tennantite vs tetrahedrite

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with tetrahedrite. Epithermal/porphyry transition mineral that feeds the entire downstream arsenate chain when it oxidizes.

tennantite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with tetrahedrite, As endmember fahlore).

habit_variants.name: prismatic_tabular
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ — pseudo-cubic tabular {001}+{110} blocks, transparent to pearly

habit_variants.name: hopper_growth
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — stepped/terraced {001} faces, skeletal hopper habit

habit_variants.name: druzy_crust
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ — fine-grained drusy coating, sparkling colorless

habit_variants.name: chalcedony_pseudomorph
habit_variants.trigger: low σ — chalcedony pseudomorph after earlier zeolite blade (stilbite/scolecite)

apophyllite.description: KCa₄Si₈O₂₀(F,OH)·8H₂O — a tetragonal sheet silicate (technically phyllosilicate) classed with the zeolites for its hydrated, low-T vesicle-filling behavior. Stage III Deccan Traps mineral per Ottens et al. 2019: forms 21–58 Ma post-eruption when alkaline groundwater finally percolates through cooled basalt vesicles. Pseudo-cubic tabular blocks with perfect {001} cleavage. Hematite-needle phantom inclusions produce the prized 'bloody apophyllite' variety from Nashik.

trace_ingredients.Fe: hematite needle phantom inclusions ('bloody apophyllite' from Nashik)
trace_ingredients.OH: OH/F substitution at the F site

apophyllite.thermal_decomp_reaction: KCa4Si8O20(F,OH)·8H2O → dehydration (loses 8 lattice waters; phyllosilicate framework collapses)

acid_dissolution.reaction: alkaline-stable phase — dissolves in acid releasing K, Ca, SiO2, F

bloody_apophyllite.trigger: hematite needle phantom inclusions (Fe>8 + O2>0.2 during growth)

nucleation_preferences.note: Nashik bloody apophyllite forms when apophyllite nucleates on/grows around pre-existing hematite needles. Requires alkaline pH (7-10), low-pressure vesicle conditions (<0.5 kbar), K+Ca+Si+F-rich fluid.

apophyllite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: zeolite-facies basalt vesicle filling, hematite phantom inclusion mechanic, deccan_zeolite scenario added).

feldspar.description: Potassium feldspar — the most abundant mineral in Earth's crust. The polymorph records T: sanidine (>500°C) → orthoclase (300-500°C) → microcline (<300°C, with diagnostic cross-hatched twinning). Pb substitution for K creates amazonite, the green pegmatite feldspar.

acid_dissolution.reaction: kaolinization — KAlSi3O8 + H+ -> kaolinite + K+ + SiO2

albite.description: NaAlSi₃O₈, the sodium end-member of the plagioclase series. Platy cleavelandite is the pegmatite signature habit. Fine albite/oligoclase exsolution lamellae scatter light into blue-white adularescence — the moonstone shimmer.
albite.audit_status: ok — all 3 runtimes (phase-3). Pegmatite albite + perthite association wired in each.

selenite.description: CaSO₄·2H₂O, the dihydrate form of gypsum — glassy, water-clear, soft enough to cleave with a fingernail. Stable only below ~60°C, where anhydrite takes over. Naica's cathedral blades grew at a steady 58°C for half a million years, producing the largest natural crystals on Earth.
selenite.audit_status: ok — all 3 runtimes (phase-3). Naica giant crystals at T~58°C for 500,000 years; above 60°C anhydrite wins.

goethite.description: FeO(OH), rust with a crystal name. Low-T oxidation product of Fe-sulfides and Fe²⁺ fluids. Velvety-black botryoidal surfaces and the classic boxwork pseudomorph after pyrite — the Egyptian Prophecy Stones are goethite after marcasite. Dehydrates to hematite above 300°C.

nucleation_preferences.note: classic weathering rind; Egyptian Prophecy Stones = goethite after marcasite

goethite.audit_status: ok (phase-1: ghost fix complete — grow_goethite + _narrate_goethite + pseudomorph-after-sulfide nucleation in all 3 runtimes).

habit_variants.name: prismatic_pyramidal
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, steady growth, moderate T

habit_variants.name: columnar_f_rich
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, F-rich fluid, moderate T

habit_variants.name: default_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: default — prismatic with steep pyramidal termination

topaz.description: Al₂SiO₄(F,OH)₂ — orthorhombic, prismatic with steep pyramidal terminations and perfect basal cleavage {001}. Imperial topaz from Ouro Preto crystallized at ~360°C, 3.5 kbar from metamorphic hydrothermal fluids (Morteani et al. 2002). Golden-orange to pinkish color from trace Cr³⁺ sourced from nearby ultramafic bodies. Colorless to pale blue when Cr is absent. F/OH ratio in the crystal records fluid composition at growth time. Crystals can snap cleanly along basal cleavage — pocket floors littered with cleavage fragments are diagnostic.

trace_ingredients.Cr: imperial golden-orange (>3 ppm), pink (>8 ppm with radiation)

acid_dissolution.reaction: very resistant — only strong mineral acids attack Al₂SiO₄(F,OH)₂ slowly

topaz.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant B, Ouro Preto Imperial Topaz). F-threshold gated nucleation; Cr³⁺ trace drives imperial color. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: striated_prism_schorl
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, high T, Fe²⁺-dominant early growth

habit_variants.name: striated_prism_elbaite
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, moderate T, Li-dominant late growth

tourmaline.description: Complex cyclosilicate — trigonal, elongated prisms with deep vertical striations and a slightly rounded triangular cross-section. Schorl (black, Fe²⁺-dominant) crystallizes early from pegmatite melt; elbaite (colored, Li-dominant) crystallizes late from residual pocket fluid as Fe depletes and Li accumulates. Striations are growth records — each ridge is a growth pulse. Color is a fluid composition snapshot: Mn²⁺ → pink (rubellite), Cr³⁺/V³⁺ → green (verdelite), Fe²⁺+Ti → blue (indicolite), Cu²⁺ → neon Paraíba blue (extreme rarity). The Jonas mine in Minas Gerais was the world's greatest rubellite producer. The most Minas Gerais mineral there is.

acid_dissolution.reaction: extremely resistant — no practical acid dissolution

tourmaline.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant A, tourmaline schorl→elbaite series). First B consumer in the sim. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: hexagonal_prism
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, steady growth, moderate T

habit_variants.name: elongated_prism
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, high T, sustained Be supply

habit_variants.name: doubly_terminated
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, moderate T, free-growing pocket

habit_variants.name: default_hex_prism
habit_variants.trigger: default — hexagonal prism with flat basal pinacoid

beryl.description: Be₃Al₂Si₆O₁₈ — hexagonal cyclosilicate, prismatic crystals that can exceed a meter in length. Beryllium is the most incompatible common element in magmatic systems: it refuses to fit into any rock-forming mineral and accumulates in residual pegmatite fluid until beryl finally nucleates. That's why the crystals are enormous — by the time the threshold is reached, there's a lot of Be waiting to crystallize. Color requires trace elements from country rock, not from the pegmatite itself: Fe²⁺ → aquamarine (blue), Mn²⁺ → morganite (pink), Cr³⁺/V³⁺ → emerald (green). The emerald paradox: you need BOTH pegmatite fluid (Be source) AND ultramafic wall rock (Cr source) to meet.

trace_ingredients.Cs: red beryl (extremely rare, not tracked here)

acid_dissolution.reaction: resistant to most acids; dissolves slowly in HF

green_emerald.trigger: Cr > 0.5 ppm or V > 1 ppm (ultramafic contact)

beryl.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant A, beryl). First Be consumer — Be is the incompatible element that accumulates forever until beryl threshold. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: tabular_prism_book
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, moderate T, Li available

habit_variants.name: elongated_blade
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, high T, sustained Li supply

spodumene.description: LiAlSi₂O₆ — monoclinic pyroxene. Flattened tabular prisms that read as 'book' shape, with two cleavage directions at ~87° producing characteristic parting. Can reach 14 meters — among the longest single crystals on Earth (Etta mine, South Dakota). Lithium accumulates late in pegmatite crystallization because no early mineral takes it, so spodumene is the residual-fluid signature. Kunzite (pink-lilac) from Mn²⁺ substitution shows strong pink-orange SW fluorescence. Hiddenite (green) from Cr³⁺ is rarer. Named for George Kunz, Tiffany's mineralogist, who bought Minas Gerais specimens by the crate.

fluorescence.note: kunzite variety; hiddenite shows weaker orange-red

spodumene.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant A, spodumene). First Li consumer — Li has been tracked as pegmatite trace since phase-2 but had no sink until now. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: prismatic_orthorhombic
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, steady growth

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_galena
habit_variants.trigger: low-moderate σ, low T, galena host

anglesite.description: PbSO₄ — orthorhombic, brilliant adamantine luster (one of the most lustrous non-metallic minerals). Intermediate step in the lead oxidation sequence: galena → anglesite → cerussite. Forms at the first wave of oxidation when sulfide-S becomes sulfate-S; transient in carbonate groundwater, where it dissolves and reprecipitates as cerussite. Named for Anglesey island (Wales) where the type specimens were found.

fluorescence.note: inconsistent, many specimens show no response

acid_dissolution.reaction: PbSO₄ + 2H⁺ → Pb²⁺ + HSO₄⁻ (slow, only strong acid)

anglesite.audit_status: new (supergene round 1, lead oxidation sequence). Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: stellate_sixling_twin
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, low T, cyclic twinning

habit_variants.name: acicular_needles

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_galena
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, galena host

cerussite.description: PbCO₃ — orthorhombic lead carbonate. Water-clear with adamantine luster and extreme birefringence (a thin slice shows doubled images). Final stable product of the lead oxidation sequence in carbonate-rich environments: galena → anglesite → cerussite. Stellate six-ray twin (cyclic twin on {110}) is among mineralogy's most iconic forms — three individuals intergrown at 120° angles producing a perfect star. Named for Latin 'cerussa' (white lead).
cerussite.audit_status: new (supergene round 1, lead oxidation sequence). Classic cyclic twins. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: hexagonal_barrel
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, steady growth

habit_variants.name: hoppered_hexagonal
habit_variants.trigger: moderate-high σ, low T, rapid edge growth

habit_variants.name: acicular_needle
habit_variants.trigger: very high σ, low T, nucleation burst

pyromorphite.description: Pb₅(PO₄)₃Cl — hexagonal apatite-group phosphate. Barrel-shaped prisms in olive-green, yellow-green, brown, or orange. Forms in supergene oxidation zones when phosphate-bearing meteoric water meets existing Pb minerals — galena, anglesite, cerussite can all be pseudomorphed. Apatite-group solid-solution end-member: pyromorphite–mimetite–vanadinite is one of mineralogy's most complete compositional series. Low toxicity (stable Pb) — pyromorphite is used in environmental remediation of Pb-contaminated soils by feeding the soil phosphate.
pyromorphite.audit_status: new (supergene round 1). First P consumer in the sim. Apatite-group cousin of existing mimetite + new vanadinite. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: hexagonal_prism_hairlike
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, slow growth

vanadinite.description: Pb₅(VO₄)₃Cl — hexagonal apatite-group vanadate. Bright red-orange to brown-orange hexagonal prisms, one of the most recognizable minerals in a collector case. Vanadate end-member of the pyromorphite-mimetite-vanadinite series. Vanadium comes from oxidation of V-bearing sediments (red-bed ironstones, roll-fronts) in arid climates — the ACF mines of Morocco and the Arizona desert have produced the finest specimens. Classified here under phosphate (apatite-group structure); the 12-hue palette has no vanadate class so we fold with P/As/V together.
vanadinite.audit_status: new (supergene round 1). First V consumer in the sim. Apatite-group cousin of pyromorphite + mimetite. Folded into phosphate class (no vanadate hue in 12-wheel). Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, high T, order-disorder above 228°C

habit_variants.name: massive_granular_low_T
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, low-moderate T, ordered below 228°C

habit_variants.name: peacock_iridescent
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, oxidation boundary

bornite.description: Cu₅FeS₄ — the 228°C order-disorder transition mineral. Above 228°C, Cu and Fe randomly occupy the cation sites (pseudo-cubic, high-T form); below, they order (orthorhombic). Fresh bronze-copper color on a broken surface, but the mineral tarnishes rapidly to the iridescent blue/purple/green/gold 'peacock ore' from thin-film interference on sulfide surface products. Forms both hypogene (hydrothermal, ~200–500°C) and supergene (cooler, as an enrichment mineral replacing chalcopyrite). Competes with chalcopyrite for Cu+Fe+S at different Cu:Fe ratios.

acid_dissolution.reaction: Cu₅FeS₄ + O₂ + acid → Cu²⁺ + Fe³⁺ + S (supergene oxidation)

bornite.audit_status: new (supergene round 2, Cu-sulfide suite). Competes with chalcopyrite. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: tabular_prismatic

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_cuFe_sulfide

habit_variants.name: massive_sooty
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T, supergene enrichment blanket

chalcocite.description: Cu₂S — 79.8% Cu by weight, one of the richest copper ores. The pseudomorph thief: replaces chalcopyrite and bornite atom-by-atom in the supergene enrichment blanket, keeping the host's outline while swapping the chemistry. Forms dark gray to black metallic crystals. Pseudo-hexagonal stellate twins (cyclic sixlings on {110}) are the collector's prize. The supergene enrichment zone is where mineable copper ore gets made — weathering concentrates Cu into a layer richer than the primary sulfide above it.
chalcocite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Cu₂S (high-T cubic) ↔ Cu₂S (low-T orthorhombic) phase transition at 103°C
chalcocite.audit_status: new (supergene round 2). Pseudomorph thief after chalcopyrite/bornite. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: thin_hex_plate

habit_variants.name: iridescent_coating

covellite.description: CuS — indigo-blue, the only common naturally blue mineral. Micaceous perfect basal cleavage (peels into flexible sheets like mica). Higher S:Cu ratio than chalcocite (1:1 vs 1:2), so it forms where sulfide activity is higher — at the boundary between the supergene oxidation zone and the reduced primary sulfides below. Decomposes to chalcocite + sulfur at 507°C. The name is Latin 'covellite' (for Niccolo Covelli, who discovered the Vesuvius fumarole specimens in 1833).
covellite.audit_status: new (supergene round 2). The only common naturally blue mineral. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: pseudomorph_after_azurite
habit_variants.trigger: Si arrives after pCO₂ drop — replaces azurite prisms

habit_variants.name: enamel_on_cuprite
habit_variants.trigger: thin film over earlier cuprite — Bisbee signature

habit_variants.name: silica_gel_hemisphere
habit_variants.trigger: high SiO₂, low σ, rounded gel drops

chrysocolla.description: Cu₂H₂Si₂O₅(OH)₄, the cyan-blue copper silicate. Cryptocrystalline and X-ray amorphous — technically a mineraloid, though sanctioned as a mineral since 1803. Forms at 5–60 °C where meteoric groundwater carries Cu²⁺ out of a weathering copper deposit and picks up SiO₂ from silicate wall rock. At Bisbee it crusts over cuprite and native copper and famously pseudomorphs azurite once pCO₂ drops and silica floods in.
chrysocolla.thermal_decomp_reaction: chrysocolla dehydrates above ~100 °C, tending toward plancheite/shattuckite

acid_dissolution.reaction: chrysocolla dissolves in strong acid — Cu²⁺ + silicic acid released

chrysocolla.audit_status: new — Bisbee scenario centrepiece, azurite-pseudomorph pCO₂-drop mechanic

habit_variants.name: octahedral
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, slow growth

habit_variants.name: chalcotrichite_plush
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T, rapid directional growth

cuprite.description: Cu₂O — 88.8% Cu by weight. Forms at the Eh boundary between native copper (more reducing) and tenorite/malachite (more oxidizing): a narrow redox window controls its existence. Dark red to nearly black with ruby-red internal reflections (from refraction, not fluorescence). Classic habits include octahedral crystals, 'chalcotrichite' hair-like plush, and massive 'tile ore'. Often occurs with native copper and malachite in supergene Cu deposits.
cuprite.audit_status: new (supergene round 3, Cu oxide/native suite). Forms at the narrow Eh window between reducing and oxidizing. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: deep_blue_prismatic
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, high pCO₂

habit_variants.name: azurite_sun_disc
habit_variants.trigger: moderate-high σ, low T, thin fracture

azurite.description: Cu₃(CO₃)₂(OH)₂ — deep azure to midnight blue, the deepest blue in the common mineral kingdom. Forms in supergene oxidation zones where high pCO₂ groundwater meets Cu²⁺. Most azurite is born to become malachite: CO₂ escapes from solution over time and the higher-carbonate azurite converts to lower-carbonate malachite, preserving the original crystal shape as a green pseudomorph. Requires carbonate country rock (limestone host) for the high pCO₂ conditions.
azurite.audit_status: new (supergene round 3). Azurite→malachite conversion mechanic implemented via CO3 depletion check in grow_azurite. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: cubic_dodecahedral
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, open vesicle

habit_variants.name: wire_copper
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, narrow channel

habit_variants.name: massive_sheet
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T, large void fill

native_copper.description: Cu — elemental native copper. Only forms when S²⁻ is absent (otherwise precipitates as sulfide) AND Eh is strongly reducing (otherwise oxidizes to cuprite/malachite). The Michigan Keweenaw peninsula basalt vesicles produced 500-ton masses from basalt-hosted brines. Copper-red fresh, tarnishes brown, eventually green (malachite coating). The Statue of Liberty's iconic patina IS malachite growing on native copper — the reverse of supergene ore formation, where rain and air turn metal into mineral in front of us.
native_copper.thermal_decomp_reaction: Cu melts at 1083°C (mineral itself stable well below)
native_copper.audit_status: new (supergene round 3). First native-element mineral. Shares native-hue class_color with future native bismuth. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: rhombic_dodecahedral
habit_variants.trigger: moderate σ, moderate T, with mineralizers

habit_variants.name: granular_massive
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, high T, rapid growth

magnetite.description: Fe₃O₄ — mixed-valence Fe²⁺Fe³⁺₂O₄ spinel oxide. Black, metallic-to-submetallic, strongly magnetic (lodestone is natural permanent-magnet magnetite). Forms at the hematite-magnetite (HM) redox buffer — one of geochemistry's most important reference frames. Crossing the HM boundary transforms the entire mineral assemblage. Octahedral {111} and rhombic dodecahedral {110} are the classic habits; martite pseudomorph is magnetite after oxidation to hematite at the crystal surface. Streak: black (diagnostic against hematite's red streak).
magnetite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Fe₃O₄ oxidizes to Fe₂O₃ above 800°C in air (martite)
magnetite.audit_status: new (round 4, iron + Sb-Bi + misc). Mixed-valence Fe²⁺Fe³⁺ oxide at the HM redox buffer. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: fibrous_micaceous
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, low T, rapid oxidation

lepidocrocite.description: γ-FeOOH — the ruby-red hydroxide dimorph of goethite. Same formula, different crystal structure: goethite is a 3D framework (yellow-brown needles), lepidocrocite is layered (ruby-red micaceous plates). Particle size controls the hue — nanoscale plates appear pink to mauve, coarser crystals rust-red. Kinetically favored over goethite under RAPID oxidation of Fe²⁺ (e.g., pyrite weathering in situ); slow oxidation gives goethite instead. Transforms to goethite over geological time or to maghemite/hematite when heated. 'Lithium quartz' sold in rock shops is actually nanoscale lepidocrocite included in quartz.
lepidocrocite.audit_status: new (round 4). γ-FeOOH dimorph of goethite, kinetically favored in rapid oxidation. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: elongated_prism_blade
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, moderate T, steady growth

stibnite.description: Sb₂S₃ — orthorhombic antimony sulfide, lead-gray to steel-gray with brilliant metallic luster on fresh cleavage. One of the most visually striking minerals: sword-like elongated blades up to 60 cm at Ichinokawa (Japan). Same structure as bismuthinite (Bi₂S₃) — Sb and Bi are geochemical cousins. Low-melting (550°C), so stibnite is destroyed by minor metamorphism. Primary Sb ore. Dendritic growth when rapidly deposited; single sword-blades when slow.
stibnite.audit_status: new (round 4). First Sb consumer. Not tied to existing scenario — needs future hydrothermal scenario with Sb-rich fluid. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: acicular_needle
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, low T, slow growth

habit_variants.name: radiating_cluster
habit_variants.trigger: moderate-high σ, moderate T, nucleation burst

bismuthinite.description: Bi₂S₃ — orthorhombic bismuth sulfide, same structure as stibnite. Lead-gray to tin-white metallic, often showing yellowish/iridescent tarnish. Forms in high-T hydrothermal veins associated with arsenopyrite, chalcopyrite, cassiterite, wolframite — the tin-tungsten-arsenic greisen suite. Temperature-driven habit switch: acicular needles at T < 350°C, stout prisms at T > 350°C. When S²⁻ runs out before Bi, switches to native bismuth.
bismuthinite.audit_status: new (round 4). First Bi consumer. Same structure as stibnite. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

habit_variants.name: massive_granular
habit_variants.trigger: high σ, moderate T, massive fill

habit_variants.name: rhombohedral_crystal
habit_variants.trigger: low σ, moderate T, open vug (rare)

native_bismuth.description: Bi — elemental native bismuth. Silver-white on fresh surface, iridescent oxide tarnish (yellow, blue, pink) develops rapidly. Melts at an unusually low 271.5°C — the lowest of any native metal. Forms only when sulfur runs out before bismuth does — bismuthinite uses the available S first, then residual Bi crystallizes native. The rainbow 'hoppered' Bi crystals sold in rock shops are lab-grown, not natural; natural native bismuth is typically arborescent/massive/rarely rhombohedral.
native_bismuth.thermal_decomp_reaction: Bi melts at 271.5°C — the lowest melting native metal

iridescent_tarnish.trigger: exposed to air — rainbow oxide film

native_bismuth.audit_status: new (round 4). Second native element. Forms after bismuthinite when S runs out. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

clinobisvanite.description: BiVO₄ — bright yellow to orange-yellow monoclinic bismuth vanadate. End of the bismuth oxidation sequence: bismuthinite → native bismuth → bismite/bismutite → clinobisvanite (if V is present). Microscopic — individual crystals rarely exceed 0.1 mm, so clinobisvanite appears as a powdery to micro-platy coating on matrix. Famously, BiVO₄ is a photocatalyst for water splitting — the same mineral that forms as a supergene afterthought is being engineered to make solar fuel. Folded into phosphate class_color (#13eb7f) per the 12-hue palette decision.
clinobisvanite.audit_status: new (round 4). Bi-vanadate — folded into phosphate class_color per 12-hue palette. End of the Bi oxidation sequence. Photocatalyst for water-splitting research. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.
